---
title: "Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema"
description: "Study note on Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema in Practical Program Evaluation."
book_id: "practical-program-evaluation"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Major section"
source_pages: "79"
citation_key: "chen_2015_practical_program_evaluation"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:practical-program-evaluation:ch03-logic-models-and-the-action-model-change-model-schema-program-theory:c0001"
source_status: "needs_review"
---
## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2015</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Source pages 79</span><span>Major section</span></div>
This note summarises the MCP research-library section "Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema" from Huey T. Chen's *Practical Program Evaluation* [@chen_2015_practical_program_evaluation]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked retrieval records, not a substitute for the source text.

## Core Argument

The chapter positions logic models and the action model/change model schema as complementary tools for describing interventions.

## Study Summary

The chapter positions logic models and the action model/change model schema as complementary tools for describing interventions.

Logic models help simplify a complicated program into components, while the schema gives evaluators a fuller way to address contextual factors and causal mechanisms.

The distinction matters because many evaluations fail when the representation is too simple for the program's actual operating logic.

## Key Concepts

- Logic model
- Action model
- Change model
- Contextual factors
- Causal mechanisms

## Source Location

- Section path: Part I: Introduction > Chapter 3: Logic Models and the Action Model/change Model Schema (program Theory)
- Evidence records: `chunk:practical-program-evaluation:ch03-logic-models-and-the-action-model-change-model-schema-program-theory:c0001`

## Practical Implications

- Use logic models for communication and initial structure.
- Use the action model/change model schema when implementation arrangements and causal assumptions require more detail.
- Choose the representation that supports evaluation decisions rather than the one stakeholders already know best.

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Concepts: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Map: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What is the main evaluation problem that Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema helps diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before using this guidance in a live evaluation?
3. How would this change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, or reporting?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## References
